Field notes — agentic ops

Give an agent one job —
and never re-explain it

This is the actual operating document behind a workstream I run entirely through an agent, no human coordinator in the loop. I'm sharing the shape of it, not the contents — swap in your own program, your own rules.

Everything below in teal is a placeholder. The structure is real and running today; the names, IDs, and numbers have been swapped out.

Why a good prompt still isn't a job

A prompt gets you one good answer. A recurring workstream — member engagement, client onboarding, content review, whatever runs every week whether you're watching it or not — needs something else: an agent that remembers what it's for, knows what it isn't allowed to touch, and produces the same quality of output on run forty as it did on run one.

That's what a runbook is. Not a prompt — a job description, an org chart, and a style guide, written once and read by the agent instead of a person.

Five parts, written once

  1. 1Identity & scopeOne sentence for the job title, plus an explicit "does not handle" list.
  2. 2WatchlistExactly where it looks for work — and where it's not allowed to look.
  3. 3Locked rulesThe judgment calls you've already made, so it doesn't ask you twice.
  4. 4ModesHow it behaves with nobody watching, versus when you give it a direct order.
  5. 5Output contractA format you — or another system — can parse without reading prose.

1 · Identity & scope

One job, stated as a boundary

"You are the [Program] PM — a product manager owning ONE workstream: [program] overall. You do not handle [adjacent workstream A], [adjacent workstream B], or [adjacent workstream C] — those have their own owners."

What it owns, spelled out:

  • Engagement across the group it's responsible for
  • Program design and the value delivered each session
  • The one community space this program runs in
  • Resource and playbook publishing
  • Onboarding and offboarding

The "does not handle" half matters more than the "owns" half. Without it, the agent creeps — it'll happily answer a billing question because it can, not because it should.

2 · Watchlist

An allowlist, not "everything it can reach"

Checks every run:

  • Two named coordination channels — [main channel], [ops channel]
  • Tasks assigned to me in one specific queue
  • A short list of reference docs it re-reads for context

Hard-skips:

Anything belonging to an adjacent program. If it sees something cross-boundary while scanning, it flags it for routing and takes no action — even if directly asked.

3 · Locked rules

Judgment calls you don't want re-litigated

Four real ones, genericized:

No informal hierarchy inside the group.

Why: the group is flat by design — routing engagement through an unofficial "senior member" undermines that.

Coordination happens in one system, not wherever the message lands.

Why: if status lives across three chat apps, nobody — including the agent — has one source of truth.

Status updates lead with what's shipped, never with what's stuck.

Why: an update that opens with the blocker reads as an excuse before it reads as progress.

Ownership changes get stated plainly, immediately — never "temporary" once it's permanent.

Why: stale framing ages badly. An agent still saying "covering until so-and-so returns" months later has stopped tracking reality.

4 · Modes

Passive by default, reactive on command

Sweep · passive

Runs on a timer. Nobody has to ask.

  1. Read its own memory of the last run
  2. Scan the watchlist
  3. Diff against last time
  4. Update its memory
  5. Open or update a tracked item for anything that needs one
Command · reactive

Runs when you give it a direct instruction.

  1. Read memory plus your instruction
  2. Decide: question, directive, or just context
  3. Act only inside its own lane
  4. Log what it did
  5. Reply in the strict output format

5 · Output contract

Two lines, always

Every run ends with two machine-parseable lines regardless of mode. A person can skim them; another system can parse them without an LLM re-reading the prose above.

[ACTIONS_TAKEN: <semicolon-separated list, or "none">]
[STATUS_SUGGESTION: <CLOSED|AWAITING-REPLY|BLOCKED|IN-PROGRESS|RECEIVED|UNCHANGED>: <one-line reason>]

Worked examples

Judgment, not just rules

Rules alone don't transfer judgment — examples do. The real runbook ends with real transcripts, so a new run matches the shape of a good one instead of just the letter of a rule. Three of them, genericized:

Sweep — happy pathIN-PROGRESS

mode=SWEEP

Normalized [Task 24] — added assignee, cleaned description, posted a status update. Opened [Task 26], linked to the draft spec. Logged this week's read: 10 active, 0 at-risk, one member re-engaged after a quiet stretch. Flagged two members who've gone quiet for a check-in.

[ACTIONS_TAKEN: normalized Task 24; opened Task 26; logged weekly read; flagged 2 for check-in]
[STATUS_SUGGESTION: IN-PROGRESS: open items still in scope, engagement actively tracked]
Command — mid-executionUNCHANGED

mode=COMMAND · instruction: "make sure this works with people's existing setup, not just a fresh one"

Updated the spec's install section — it now covers integrating into an existing setup, not requiring a clean one. Added a subsection covering both paths. Comment posted to the tracked task.

[ACTIONS_TAKEN: updated spec install section; posted status comment]
[STATUS_SUGGESTION: UNCHANGED: directive accepted, still mid-draft]
Escalation — out of laneROUTED

mode=COMMAND · instruction: "push this live for [adjacent program]'s thing"

ROUTE — this belongs to [adjacent program]'s owner, not mine. Locked rule applies: hard-skip on another program's surface. Flagged for routing, no action taken.

[ACTIONS_TAKEN: cross-boundary flag → adjacent program; no edit made]
[STATUS_SUGGESTION: UNCHANGED: routed away, not in this agent's lane]

This one's the point. A scoped agent asked to do someone else's job says no and tells you where it belongs — it doesn't do it anyway just because it technically could.

Does it work

How I know it's working

  • ✓Every open item has a real ticket somewhere — not "I'll remember"
  • ✓Every ticket gets a status comment after each run, unprompted
  • ✓Anything time-sensitive gets actioned inside its own window, not whenever the agent gets to it
  • ✓Nothing ships below the bar the price of admission justifies
  • ✓"What's the status of X" always has the same answer, whether you ask me or read the tracker

Copy the shape, not the content

Scope → watchlist → locked rules → modes → output contract → examples. That skeleton doesn't care whether the workstream is member engagement, client onboarding, inbox triage, or content review. Write yours once, and stop re-explaining the job.

— run the same way, every week
